parentQuestion: A patient who has experienced previous jaw and face trauma now reports difficulty tasting with the tip of her tongue. Which cranial nerve was likely damaged in that injury?

scaffolds.q: Taste sensation from different parts of the tongue travels via different cranial nerves.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The tongue isn't innervated by just one nerve for taste.

scaffolds.q: The ANTERIOR 2/3 of the tongue (including tip) → CN VII (facial nerve).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Specifically via the chorda tympani branch of CN VII.

scaffolds.q: The POSTERIOR 1/3 of the tongue → CN IX (glossopharyngeal).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Different nerve for the back of tongue taste.

scaffolds.q: CN V (trigeminal) provides TOUCH sensation to tongue, not TASTE.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! CN V = general sensation (pain, touch, temperature). CN VII/IX = taste.

scaffolds.q: CN XII (hypoglossal) controls tongue MOVEMENT, not taste.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Hypoglossal is motor only. Taste is sensory via VII and IX.

scaffolds.q: The chorda tympani (CN VII) passes near the jaw and middle ear.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This makes it vulnerable to jaw/face trauma.

scaffolds.q: Tip of tongue = anterior tongue = CN VII territory.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Loss of taste at tip = CN VII (facial nerve) damage.

scaffolds.q: Taste from tongue tip = anterior 2/3 = CN VII (facial nerve via chorda tympani).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Jaw trauma can damage this nerve's chorda tympani branch.

scaffolds.q: The answer is FACIAL (VII) - carries taste from anterior 2/3 of tongue.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not CN V (touch, not taste), not CN XII (motor, not taste), not CN IX (posterior 1/3, not tip). Facial nerve via chorda tympani carries anterior tongue taste.
